source: Acumen Fuse / DCMA 14-Point Schedule Assessment standard thresholds
version: Fuse-standard, as published in the Defense Contract Management Agency 14-Point Schedule Assessment guidance and reproduced in the Acumen Fuse Metrics Library default ruleset.

S1_logic.name: Logic
S1_logic.description: Detail tasks missing predecessors or successors. Project start and project finish milestones are exempt.
S1_logic.rationale: Each detail task should have at least one predecessor (except project start) and at least one successor (except project finish). Higher values indicate a poorly networked schedule that cannot reliably forecast slip.

S2_leads.name: Leads (negative lag)
S2_leads.rationale: Negative lag (a lead) implies a successor can start before its predecessor finishes, which contradicts the FS relationship semantics and is generally considered bad practice. DCMA tolerates none.

S3_lags.name: Lags (positive lag)
S3_lags.rationale: Positive lags hide work — the gap should usually be modeled as an explicit task. DCMA tolerates up to 5% of relationships carrying lag before flagging.

S4_relationship_types.name: Relationship Types
S4_relationship_types.description: Percentage of relationships that are Finish-to-Start (FS).
S4_relationship_types.rationale: Non-FS relationships (SS, FF, SF) are harder to interpret in a time-impact analysis and can mask sequencing issues. DCMA requires at least 90% of relationships to be FS.

S5_hard_constraints.name: Hard Constraints
S5_hard_constraints.description: Detail tasks carrying a hard constraint (Must-Start-On, Must-Finish-On, Start-No-Later-Than, Finish-No-Later-Than, etc.).
S5_hard_constraints.rationale: Hard constraints override CPM float calculations and are a classic vector for manipulation. DCMA tolerates up to 5% of detail tasks carrying constraints; anything more flags.

S6_high_float.name: High Float
S6_high_float.description: Incomplete detail tasks with total float greater than 44 working days (approximately two months).
S6_high_float.rationale: Excessive float indicates either missing logic, an inflated baseline, or unresourced parallel work. DCMA tolerates up to 5% of incomplete tasks above the 44-day ceiling.

S7_negative_float.name: Negative Float
S7_negative_float.description: Detail tasks with negative total float.
S7_negative_float.rationale: Negative float means the schedule is forecasting a miss against its driving constraint. DCMA tolerates none — the schedule should always be feasible against its constraints, even if the constraint date later changes.

S8_high_duration.name: High Duration
S8_high_duration.description: Incomplete detail tasks with working duration greater than 44 working days (approximately two months).
S8_high_duration.rationale: Long-duration tasks aggregate too much work and obscure progress reporting. The 44-day ceiling forces the planner to break long activities into reportable chunks.

S9_invalid_dates.name: Invalid Dates
S9_invalid_dates.description: Tasks whose actual_start or actual_finish date falls after the schedule status date, or whose forecast finish falls before the status date.
S9_invalid_dates.rationale: Actuals after the status date are temporally impossible; forecasts before the status date suggest the schedule was not properly updated. DCMA tolerates none.

S10_resources.name: Resources
S10_resources.description: Incomplete detail tasks without any resource assignments (labor, equipment, or material).
S10_resources.rationale: Unresourced tasks cannot drive earned-value or cost-impact analysis. DCMA tolerates up to 5% of incomplete tasks lacking resources.

S11_missed_tasks.name: Missed Tasks
S11_missed_tasks.description: Tasks whose baseline finish date is on or before the status date but which are not yet 100% complete.
S11_missed_tasks.rationale: Tasks that should have finished but did not are direct evidence of slip. DCMA tolerates up to 5% of detail tasks missing their baseline finish before the schedule starts to accumulate as-built variance flags.

S12_critical_path_test.name: Critical Path Test
S12_critical_path_test.description: Adding a long delay (typically 600 days) to a critical-path task should extend the project finish by exactly that amount. If it does not, the critical path is broken or constraint-pinned.
S12_critical_path_test.rationale: A working CPM network propagates change end-to-end. A failed CPT indicates open-ended successors, broken logic, or hard constraints that prevent the network from responding to delay.

S13_critical_path_length_index.name: Critical Path Length Index (CPLI)
S13_critical_path_length_index.description: Ratio of (critical path length + total float) to critical path length. Values >= 1.0 mean the schedule has float; <1.0 means the schedule has lost float against the contractual end date.
S13_critical_path_length_index.rationale: Fuse-standard threshold is 0.95 — i.e. the schedule is allowed to be at most 5% behind on its float position before flagging. A CPLI below 0.95 indicates compression risk.

S14_baseline_execution_index.name: Baseline Execution Index (BEI)
S14_baseline_execution_index.description: Ratio of completed-and-on-time tasks to tasks that should have been completed by the status date.
S14_baseline_execution_index.rationale: Fuse-standard threshold is 0.95 — i.e. the project is allowed to slip at most 5% of its planned completion velocity before flagging. A BEI below 0.95 is the leading indicator of cumulative slip.
